This  Catalogue  of  Bengali,  Oriya,  and  Assamese  Books, 
compiled  by  Professor  J.  F.  Blumhardt  upon  principles  which 
are  explained  in  the  Preface,  is  published  as  the  Fourth  Part 
of  the  Second  Volume,  containing  works  in  Oriental  languages, 
of  the  "  Catalogue  of  the  Library  of  the  India  Office."  The 
previous  parts  of  this  Volume  were — I.  Sanskrit  Books,  by 
Dr.  Reinhold  Rest,  1897 ;  II.  Hindustaui  Books,  by  Professor 
J.  F.  Blumhardt,  1900  ;  III.  Hindi,  PaLJabi,  Pushtu,  and  Sindhi 
Books,  by  the  same,  1902. 

F.  W.  Thomas, 

Librarian,  India  Offi.ce. 

6th  Julv  1905. 


LIBRARY 
UNIVERSITV  OF  CALIFORNIA 
SANTA  BARBARA 
PREFACE. 


The  Bengali  books  have  been  arranged  under  a  subject-classification, 
uniform  with  that  in  other  published  catalogues  of  Indian  Vernacular 
books  in  this  Library.  A  complete  list  of  the  names  of  works,  with 
a  few  subject-headings  not  appearing  in  the  classification  of  the 
catalogue,  will  be  found  at  tlie  end,  and  also  an  index  of  the  names 
of  authors  and  of  their  works  in  alphabetical  arrangement.  The  Oriya 
and  Assamese  books,  being  lew  in  number,  have  not  been  classified, 
but  are  arranged  alphabetically,  an  index  of  the  names  of  authors  and 
of  their  works  being  appended  to  each  catalogue. 

The  names  of  authors  and  the  titles  of  works  are  ranscribed 
exactly  as  they  are  spelt  in  the  vernacular,  according  to  a  definite 
system  of  transliteration  which  has  been  uniforndy  folloAved  in  the 
preparation  of  the  various  catalogues  of  Indian  Vernacular  books  in 
this  Library,  based  on  the  system  and  general  principles  adopted 
in  the  Library  of  the  British  Museum.  In  order  to  secure  uni- 
formity with  the  spelling  of  names  in  the  Sanskrit  Catalogue,  the 
final  inherent  '*a" — though  often  unsoun<led  in  the  Bengali  pro- 
nunciation of  a  Sanskrit  word — has  been  retained  ;  as,  for  instance, 
Somadeva  Bliatta,  Riimayana,  Bhagavatapurana.  In  Bengali  and 
other  xeinacular  modified  forms  of  Sanskrit  words  (fadbharas)  the 
unsounded  inherent  "  a  "  has  been  oinittt'd  ;  e.c/.,  'rekchand  Tl'^^l^i"', 
Bankimchandra. 

It  may  apixar  strange  tt>  .--cu  I'aiiiiliiir  naiucs,  siicli  ;i.s  Raja  Rani- 
nioliiin  Hoy,  Romesh  Cluuider  Dutt,  and  K.  M.  Jianerjea,  api)e;uiii;; 
under  tlieir  more  correct  forms  as  Ramariiohana  Rayii,  Ilamesachandi.i 
Datta,  and  Krishuainohaua  Vandyopadliyaya,  Imt  it,  is  obvious  tliat 
for  cataloguing  |)urposes  a  consistent  adiierence  to  an  (s.-stabH.shed 
method  of  transliteration  is  most  essential;  any  deviation  from  it 
would  result  in  much  confusion.  It  is  gratifying  to  observe  that 
modern  Bengali  authors  are  generally  adopting  a  rational  and  correct 
system  of  transliterating  their  names,  the  arbitrary  and  old-fashioned 
forms  of  spelling  according  to  the  pronunciation  being  almost  uni- 
veisally  discarded.  For  the  convenience  of  readers,  cross-references 
have  been  made  from  the  familiar,  incorrect,  or  phonetic  forms  to 
the  correct  sf>elling. 

^  I.  813.  a  2 


iv  PREFACE. 

Following  this  general  pnnci|)le,  the  names  of  Hindustani  authors, 
and  titles  of  books,  have  been,  as  far  as  possible,  transliterated 
according  to  the  Hindus-tani  spelling,  and  not  according  to  the  Bengali 
phonetic  forms,  frequently  absurdly  incorrect,  especially  in  the  case 
of  the  Bengali  literature  current  amongst  Muhammadan  readers, 
commonly  known  as  Muhammadan  Bengali. 

An  author's  true  name,  his  notnen  pruprium,  is  placed  first.  This 
generally  consists  of  a  compound  word  {scimdsa),  such  as  Chandra- 
kumara,  Harimohana,  Krishnadasa,  which  is  frequently  written  by 
Bengalis  as  two  distinct  names,  e.g.^  Chandra  Kumar,  Hari  Mohan, 
Krishna  Das.  This  is  followed  by  the  caste-title  or  degree-title,  such 
as  Sarma,  Bhattacharya,  Kaviratna.  Here  also  the  familiar  phonetic 
or  abridged  forms  of  Banerjee,  Chatterjee,  and  the  like,  are  tran- 
scribed in  strict  accordance  with  the  Bengali  spelling  of  these  names, 
viz.,  Vandyopadhyaya  and  Chattopadhyaya.  The  system  of  cata- 
loguing Bengali  names  under  the  caste-title,  regarding  it  as  a  sur- 
name, is  open  to  this  serious  objection,  that  Bengali  authors  are 
frequently  in  the  habit  of  dropping  the  caste-title,  or  of  substituting 
or  adding  a  degree-title.  Thus  we  have  Mrityuiijaya  Sarma  ap- 
pearing subsequently  as  Mrityuiijaya  Vidyalankara,  Prasannachandra 
Obakravarti  as  Prasannachandra  Vidyaratna,  Isvarachandra  Bhatta- 
charya as  Isvarachandra  Vidyasagara. 

Titles,  such  as  Munshi,  Pandit,  Baja,  and  the  like,  have  been 
generally  omitted.  In  the  Index  of  Persons,  a  title,  place  of  resi- 
dence, or  other  method  of  differentiating  one  author  frou»  another 
bearing  the  same  name,  is  placed  after  his  name  in  italics.  The 
taMlciUtis  of  Hindustani  authors  is  shown  in  parentheses  after  the 
personal  name,  a  cross-reference  being  made  in  every  case  from  it  to 
the  author's  real  name. 

J.  F.  Blumhardt. 

London,  4th  July  1905.
